message:
fix(workers/reserve_slots): refuse reservations on draining/drained/disabled workers

The build selector calls `healthy_workers()` to filter candidates, then
calls `WorkerState::reserve_slots(count)` on the chosen candidate. There
is a gap between those two calls. If an operator invokes `rch workers
drain` inside that gap, the previous implementation of `reserve_slots`
only compared `used + count` against `total_slots` and silently accepted
the reservation on the now-draining worker — so a new build could land
on a worker we had just asked to stop accepting work.

That defeats the entire drain contract: the operator runs `drain`, a
build immediately lands there, and the worker can't actually be taken
out of rotation until the build finishes (which may itself wedge if the
operator was draining to triage a worker bug).

Fix: inside the CAS loop of `reserve_slots`, re-read `self.status` on
every iteration and return `false` if the worker is `Draining`,
`Drained`, or `Disabled`. Reads the status under the same async
`RwLock` the rest of the module uses, so a concurrent `drain()` or
`disable()` becomes authoritative as soon as it's written — not only
on the next selection round.

Also swaps the bare `current + count > total_slots` comparison for
`saturating_add` — guards against wraparound if a future change ever
lets `count` approach u32::MAX (e.g. a malformed build request); the
saturated compare simply refuses the reservation instead of panicking
in debug / silently accepting in release.

Regression test `test_reserve_slots_refuses_draining_worker` walks a
single WorkerState through healthy → reserve → release → drain →
reserve(refused) → check_drain_complete → Drained → reserve(refused)
→ disable → reserve(refused), mirroring the operator's real sequence.

Adjacent test-only fixup: `test_prune_drained_workers_with_slots` had
`drain()` preceding `reserve_slots(1)` in its setup. Under the new
semantics the reservation would now refuse, so the setup is reordered
to match production sequencing: reserve *first* (while healthy), then
drain. Comment added so a future reader understands why.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
